does: This paper presents CHASE, a closed-loop adversarial red-blue teaming framework that iteratively improves LLM safety defenses against prompt-rewriting attacks. The attacker uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a multiplicative reward combining bypass effectiveness and intent fidelity to generate sophisticated adversarial prompts like persona modulation and fictional framing. The defender is simultaneously hardened using a two-stage pipeline combining GRPO and rejection-sampled supervised fine-tuning, balanced with benign data to prevent capability degradation. Evaluated on BeaverTails and JailbreakBench benchmarks, the approach demonstrates robustness against five held-out attack families including PAIR, TAP, AutoDAN, PAP, and translation-based attacks, showing that co-evolutionary hardening produces more adaptive defenses than static human-curated or white-box optimization approaches.
connects: This work builds on the emerging research in adversarial red-blue teaming for AI safety, extending prior work on jailbreaking and defense mechanisms beyond human-curated rule-based filters. It responds to limitations of earlier approaches like PAIR (prompt-based attack iterative refinement) and other gradient-free attack methods by creating a scalable closed-loop system. The paper connects to recent reinforcement learning alignment methods, specifically adapting Group Relative Policy Optimization from instruction-following work to the adversarial setting. It extends beyond white-box optimization defenses that overfit to specific model architectures, drawing on insights from adversarial machine learning that dynamic co-evolution improves robustness compared to static defenses. The multiplicative reward design reflects prior work balancing multiple objectives in safety training while maintaining semantic fidelity.
enables: CHASE establishes foundations for deployable, adaptive safety systems that improve continuously against emerging attack classes without requiring human retraining cycles. The co-evolutionary framework suggests a pathway toward self-improving safety mechanisms where systems anticipate and defend against novel adaptive adversaries at scale. Future work could extend this to multi-round interactions, application to other modalities like images or code, and integration with real-world deployment pipelines. The approach enables investigation of fundamental questions around the limits of prompt-based attacks and whether safety via rejection-sampling represents a scalable long-term solution. It opens directions for understanding the dynamics of adversarial co-evolution in language models and potentially developing formal guarantees for robustness against classes of black-box attacks, moving safety research toward systems that remain resilient as attackers themselves improve.
